spec:
Evaluate the focus distinction of the cat in the photograph. Score how sharply and clearly the cat is rendered relative to the rest of the image. What matters is the relative gap in clarity between the cat and everything else in the frame — not absolute sharpness.

A cat that is crisply focused while its surroundings fall into softness creates a natural visual hierarchy that draws the eye directly to the cat. This scores well. A photograph where everything is equally sharp offers no emphasis and no hierarchy — the cat competes with every element for the viewer's attention. This scores poorly. The worst case is when the cat itself is out of focus while the camera has locked onto something else — the photograph has actively directed attention away from the cat.

Score high when the cat is the sharpest element in the frame and the background or surroundings are noticeably softer, creating a clear depth-of-field separation. Score low when the cat is blurry, when focus is locked on a non-cat element, or when uniform sharpness across the entire image leaves the cat with no focus-based advantage. A slightly soft image where the cat is still the sharpest thing in frame should score higher than a razor-sharp image where every element is equally crisp.

Evaluate the subject prominence of the cat in the photograph. Score how much the cat occupies and commands the physical space of the frame. Determine whether the cat is large enough, and positioned purposefully enough, to register as the subject of the photograph at a glance.

A cat that fills a generous portion of the image and sits at a natural focal point scores well — it feels like the reason the photograph was taken, as though someone pointed a camera at it with deliberate intent. The cat should feel like the gravitational center of the image. A cat that is tiny, distant, cropped awkwardly, pushed to an extreme edge, or dwarfed by the scale of its surroundings scores poorly — it feels incidental, as though it wandered into a photograph that was about something else.

Score high when the cat is large in the frame, positioned centrally or at a natural point of visual emphasis, and feels like the dominant presence in the composition. Score low when the cat is small, distant, marginal in the frame, or requires the viewer to zoom in or search to appreciate. A cat photographed from across a large room, appearing as a small shape among furniture, fails on prominence regardless of other qualities. The cat must be substantial enough to register as the subject immediately at the native viewing size of the image.

Evaluate the visual separation of the cat in the photograph. Score how well the cat stands apart from its background and from any competing elements in the scene. This evaluation has two dimensions: separation from the background, and separation from competing visual elements.

For background separation, assess whether the cat's form, color, and texture create a clear boundary against its surroundings. A black cat on a black couch, a tabby on a similarly patterned rug, or a white cat against snow — these are cases where the cat camouflages into its environment and the boundaries between cat and not-cat become ambiguous. Strong background separation means the cat is visually distinct from what is behind and around it.

For competing element separation, assess whether other visual elements in the photograph — other animals, people, bright objects, bold patterns, text, or vivid colors — pull the viewer's attention away from the cat. Each competing element dilutes the cat's claim to being the subject. The cat should be visually alone in its claim to the viewer's eye.

Score high when the cat contrasts clearly against its environment and there are no other elements competing for the viewer's attention — the cat is the only thing worth looking at. Score low when the cat blends into a similarly colored or textured background, or when the scene is visually crowded with elements that rival the cat for attention. The photograph should feel as though the stage has been cleared for the cat to hold it alone.
